ttest.name: Independent & Paired T-Test
ttest.description: Compare means between two groups. Get t-value, p-value, Cohen's d, and 95% CI.

anova.name: One-Way ANOVA
anova.description: Compare means across three or more groups. F-statistic, eta-squared, and post-hoc tests.

chiSquare.name: Chi-Square Test
chiSquare.description: Test of independence and goodness-of-fit. Chi-square statistic, Cramer's V.

correlation.name: Correlation
correlation.description: Pearson r and Spearman rho. Scatter plot visualization with regression line.

descriptive.name: Descriptive Statistics
descriptive.description: Mean, median, SD, skewness, kurtosis, quartiles, and more.

sampleSize.name: Sample Size Calculator
sampleSize.description: Power analysis for t-tests, ANOVA, correlation, chi-square & proportions.

oneSampleT.name: One-Sample T-Test
oneSampleT.description: Compare a sample mean against a known or hypothesized population value.

mannWhitney.name: Mann-Whitney U Test
mannWhitney.description: Non-parametric comparison of two independent groups. Rank-based alternative to t-test.

wilcoxon.name: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank
wilcoxon.description: Non-parametric test for paired samples. Alternative to paired t-test.

regression.name: Simple Linear Regression
regression.description: Model the relationship between X and Y. Equation, R², scatter plot.

multipleRegression.name: Multiple Regression
multipleRegression.description: Predict Y from multiple predictors. R², VIF, ANOVA table, standardized betas.

cronbachAlpha.name: Cronbach's Alpha
cronbachAlpha.description: Assess internal consistency reliability. Item statistics, split-half, alpha-if-deleted.

logisticRegression.name: Logistic Regression
logisticRegression.description: Predict binary outcomes. Odds ratios, classification table, Hosmer-Lemeshow test.

factorAnalysis.name: Factor Analysis
factorAnalysis.description: Explore latent factors. KMO, eigenvalues, factor loadings, Varimax & Promax rotation.

kruskalWallis.name: Kruskal-Wallis H Test
kruskalWallis.description: Non-parametric comparison of three or more independent groups. Rank-based alternative to one-way ANOVA.

repeatedMeasures.name: Repeated Measures ANOVA
repeatedMeasures.description: Compare means across conditions measured on the same subjects. Sphericity test and post-hoc comparisons.

twoWayAnova.name: Two-Way ANOVA (Factorial)
twoWayAnova.description: Examine main effects and interaction of two factors. Interaction plot and APA-formatted results.

friedman.name: Friedman Test
friedman.description: Non-parametric alternative to repeated measures ANOVA. Compare ranks across three or more conditions.

fisherExact.name: Fisher's Exact Test
fisherExact.description: Exact test for 2×2 contingency tables. Ideal for small sample sizes where chi-square is unreliable.

mcnemar.name: McNemar Test
mcnemar.description: Test for paired nominal data. Compare proportions in before-after or matched-pair designs.

apa.title: APA 7th Edition
apa.description: Results are automatically formatted to APA standards. No more manual formatting.

instant.description: All calculations run in your browser. No server needed. Fast and private.

step1.title: Enter Your Data

trust.validatedDescription: Every calculator in StatMate has been cross-validated against R (the gold standard for statistical computing) and SPSS. Our results match to at least 4 decimal places across all test statistics, p-values, and effect sizes.

parametric.title: Parametric Tests
parametric.e: Repeated Measures ANOVA → R aov() + Error()

trust.privacyDescription: Your data never leaves your device. All statistical calculations run entirely in your browser using client-side JavaScript. No data is uploaded, stored, or logged on our servers.

home.aiSubtitle: Pro feature — see how AI explains your results in plain language

socialProof.title: Trusted by Researchers Worldwide
socialProof.t1: StatMate saved me hours of formatting. The APA output is perfect — I just copy and paste into my paper.
socialProof.t2: Finally, a free stats tool that actually gives you effect sizes and confidence intervals, not just p-values.
socialProof.t3: I recommend StatMate to all my students. It's fast, accurate, and teaches proper reporting.

stories.title: How Researchers Use StatMate
stories.s1: Used StatMate to run an independent-samples t-test for her thesis data. The APA-formatted output saved hours of manual formatting — she just copied the result directly into her paper.
stories.s2: Uses StatMate in class to demonstrate how different statistical tests work. Students enter sample data and instantly see APA-formatted results, making abstract concepts concrete.
stories.s3: Ran 5 different tests for her dissertation — t-test, ANOVA, correlation, chi-square, and regression. Exported all results to Word in APA format with one click each.

0.a: Yes! All 20 statistical calculators are completely free with no sign-up required. Pro features like AI interpretation and Word export are available for $5.99/month.

1.a: Absolutely. All calculations run entirely in your browser. Your data never leaves your device — we don't upload, store, or log any of your research data.

2.a: Every calculator is cross-validated against R and SPSS to at least 4 decimal places. We use established formulas from authoritative statistics textbooks.

3.a: APA (American Psychological Association) format is the standard reporting style used in psychology, education, and social sciences. StatMate automatically formats your results following APA 7th edition guidelines.

4.q: Can I use StatMate for my thesis or dissertation?
4.a: Yes! StatMate provides publication-ready APA formatted results that you can directly copy into your thesis, dissertation, or research paper. You can also cite StatMate using the formats on our About page.

5.a: StatMate offers 20 tests: T-tests (independent, paired, one-sample), ANOVA (one-way, two-way, repeated measures), non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney, Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis, Friedman, Fisher's exact), regression (simple, multiple, logistic), chi-square, correlation, factor analysis, Cronbach's alpha, McNemar test, and more.

ttest.metaTitle: Free T-Test Calculator Online - Independent & Paired
ttest.metaDescription: Free online t-test calculator. Independent samples and paired t-test with APA-formatted results. Get t-value, p-value, Cohen's d, and 95% CI instantly.
ttest.title: T-Test Calculator
ttest.description: Compare means between two groups using an independent or paired samples t-test. Results are formatted in APA 7th edition style.
ttest.independentDescription: Compare means from two separate, unrelated groups.
ttest.pairedDescription: Compare means from the same group at two time points (e.g., pre-test vs post-test).
ttest.errorPairedEqual: Paired t-test requires equal sample sizes in both groups.
ttest.exampleScenario: A psychologist tested whether a cognitive training program improves working memory. 10 participants completed the program (experimental), and 10 did not (control). Working memory was measured by digit span scores.
ttest.exampleScenarioPaired: A clinical researcher measured anxiety scores (1-100) of 10 patients before and after an 8-week mindfulness intervention.

anova.metaDescription: Free online one-way ANOVA calculator with APA-formatted results. F-statistic, p-value, eta-squared, and Bonferroni post-hoc tests.
anova.title: One-Way ANOVA Calculator
anova.description: Compare means across three or more groups. Results include F-statistic, p-value, eta-squared, and Bonferroni post-hoc tests in APA format.
anova.groupMinValues: Each group needs at least 2 values.
anova.exampleScenario: An education researcher compared exam scores across 3 teaching methods: traditional lecture, flipped classroom, and project-based learning. Each group had 10 students.

chiSquare.metaTitle: Free Chi-Square Test Calculator — Independence & Goodness of Fit
chiSquare.metaDescription: Free chi-square calculator for independence and goodness-of-fit tests. Get χ² statistic, p-value, Cramer's V, and APA-formatted results instantly. No signup.
chiSquare.title: Chi-Square Calculator
chiSquare.description: Test of independence for contingency tables and goodness-of-fit. Results are formatted in APA 7th edition style.
chiSquare.independenceDescription: Test whether two categorical variables are associated in a contingency table.
chiSquare.goodnessDescription: Test whether observed frequencies match expected frequencies.
chiSquare.errorMinCells: Please fill in all cells with valid numbers.
chiSquare.errorEqualLength: Observed and expected must have the same number of values.
chiSquare.exampleScenario: A market researcher surveyed 200 consumers to test whether product preference (A vs B) is independent of age group (under 30 vs 30+).
chiSquare.exampleScenarioGoodness: A geneticist tested whether observed phenotype ratios match the expected 9:3:3:1 Mendelian ratio from 160 offspring.

correlation.metaTitle: Free Correlation Calculator Online - Pearson & Spearman
correlation.metaDescription: Free Pearson and Spearman correlation calculator with scatter plot. Instant r-value, p-value, and confidence interval in APA format. No signup, no installation.
correlation.title: Correlation Calculator
correlation.description: Calculate Pearson r or Spearman rho between two variables. Results include scatter plot, p-value, and APA-formatted output.
correlation.pearsonDescription: Measure the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two continuous variables.
correlation.spearmanDescription: Measure the strength and direction of the monotonic relationship between two variables.
correlation.errorMinPairs: Each variable needs at least 3 values.
correlation.errorEqualLength: Both variables must have the same number of values.
correlation.exampleScenario: A university study examined the relationship between weekly study hours and final exam scores for 12 undergraduate students.

descriptive.metaDescription: Free online descriptive statistics calculator. Mean, median, mode, SD, skewness, kurtosis, quartiles and more with APA-formatted results.
descriptive.title: Descriptive Statistics Calculator
descriptive.description: Calculate mean, median, mode, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis, quartiles, and more. Results are formatted in APA 7th edition style.
descriptive.inputDescription: Enter a list of numbers to compute descriptive statistics.
descriptive.errorMinValues: Need at least 2 values for descriptive statistics.
descriptive.exampleScenario: A teacher collected math test scores (out of 100) from a class of 15 students to understand the overall performance distribution.

faqs.q: Can I use the calculators without signing up?
faqs.a: Yes! All calculators are completely free and require no sign-up. You can calculate t-tests, ANOVA, chi-square, correlation, and descriptive statistics instantly.

faqs.q: What does the AI interpretation include?
faqs.a: Our AI analyzes your statistical results and generates: (1) a publication-ready result sentence in APA format, (2) a plain-language interpretation anyone can understand, and (3) important caveats and limitations to consider.

faqs.q: What format is the Word export?
faqs.a: We generate APA 7th edition formatted tables in .docx format. Tables follow APA guidelines: no vertical lines, proper header formatting, italic statistical symbols (M, SD, t, F, p), and Times New Roman 12pt font.
Yes. You can cancel your subscription at any time. You'll keep Pro access until the end of your billing period.
All calculations run in your browser. Your raw data never leaves your device. For AI interpretation, only the computed statistics (not your raw data) are sent to our server.
We're planning student pricing soon. For now, the 3 free AI trials and pay-per-use option ($2.99/export) make it affordable for any student.

about.metaTitle: About StatMate — Free APA Statistics Calculator for Researchers
about.metaDescription: Learn about StatMate, a free online statistics calculator with APA-formatted results. Built by statisticians for students and researchers. 20+ calculators, PDF export, AI interpretation.
about.title: About StatMate
about.intro: <bold>StatMate</bold> is a free online statistics calculator designed for researchers, graduate students, and anyone who needs quick, accurate statistical analysis with publication-ready results.
about.mission: We believe statistical analysis should be accessible to everyone. Whether you're writing your first research paper or your hundredth, StatMate helps you get accurate results formatted in APA 7th edition style — no expensive software required.

differences.apa: <bold>APA-Ready:</bold> Results are automatically formatted following APA 7th edition guidelines. Copy and paste directly into your paper.
differences.privacy: <bold>Privacy First:</bold> All calculations run in your browser. Your raw data never leaves your device.
differences.builtBy: <bold>Built by Statisticians:</bold> Every calculator is built and verified by statistics professionals with years of research experience.
differences.free: <bold>Always Free:</bold> Core calculators are free forever. No sign-up required.

about.teamDescription: StatMate was founded by researchers who were frustrated with expensive SPSS licensing costs and the tedious process of manual APA formatting. Built by a team combining deep statistical expertise with modern web development, our mission is to make professional-grade statistical analysis accessible to every researcher.

stat.title: Statistical Accuracy
stat.description: Every calculator is cross-validated against R and SPSS to ensure results match to at least 4 decimal places. We follow established formulas from authoritative statistics textbooks.

apa.title: APA Formatting
apa.description: Our APA output follows the Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association, 7th Edition. We regularly review updates to ensure compliance.

tech.title: Modern Engineering
tech.description: Built with Next.js, TypeScript, and jStat. Client-side computation ensures privacy and speed. Open-source statistical libraries for transparency.

about.citationDescription: If you use StatMate in your research, please cite it using one of the following formats:

apa.label: APA 7th Edition
apa.text: StatMate. (2025). StatMate: Free online statistics calculator with APA-formatted results [Web application]. https://statmate.org

mla.label: MLA 9th Edition
mla.text: "StatMate: Free Online Statistics Calculator with APA-Formatted Results." StatMate, 2025, statmate.org.

chicago.label: Chicago 17th Edition
chicago.text: StatMate. "Free Online Statistics Calculator with APA-Formatted Results." Accessed 2025. https://statmate.org.

about.methodologyDescription: All 20 calculators use established statistical formulas from authoritative textbooks and are cross-validated against R and SPSS to at least 4 decimal places.

parametric.title: Parametric Tests (5)
parametric.tests: T-Test (independent, paired, one-sample), One-Way ANOVA, Two-Way ANOVA, Repeated Measures ANOVA — validated against R t.test(), aov()

nonParametric.title: Non-Parametric Tests (6)
nonParametric.tests: Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon Signed-Rank, Kruskal-Wallis H, Friedman, Chi-Square, Fisher's Exact — validated against R wilcox.test(), kruskal.test(), chisq.test(), fisher.test()

regression.title: Regression & Correlation (4)
regression.tests: Simple Regression, Multiple Regression, Logistic Regression, Pearson/Spearman Correlation — validated against R lm(), glm(), cor.test()

other.title: Reliability & Other (5)
other.tests: Cronbach's Alpha, Factor Analysis (EFA), McNemar Test, Sample Size/Power Analysis, Descriptive Statistics — validated against R psych::alpha(), factanal(), pwr package

about.methodologyNote: All calculations run client-side in your browser. Source code uses jStat, simple-statistics, and custom implementations following formulas from Howell (2013), Field (2018), and Tabachnick & Fidell (2019).
about.contactText: Have feedback, found a bug, or want to request a new calculator? We'd love to hear from you.

teamOrigin.title: Why We Built StatMate
teamOrigin.desc: As graduate researchers, we spent too many hours wrestling with SPSS licenses that expired mid-semester and manually formatting APA tables. We knew there had to be a better way. StatMate was born from that frustration — a tool built by researchers, for researchers, that combines statistical rigor with modern web technology.

t-test.paperReady: An independent samples t-test revealed a statistically significant difference between the experimental group (M = 4.52, SD = 1.23) and the control group (M = 3.81, SD = 1.45), t(58) = 2.45, p = .018, d = 0.63.
t-test.plainLanguage: The experimental group scored meaningfully higher than the control group, with a medium-sized effect. This difference is unlikely to be due to chance alone.

anova.paperReady: A one-way ANOVA revealed a statistically significant difference among the three treatment groups, F(2, 87) = 5.34, p = .007, η² = .11.
anova.plainLanguage: There was a meaningful difference between the groups. Post-hoc tests can reveal which specific groups differ from each other.

chi-square.paperReady: A chi-square test of independence indicated a significant association between gender and preference, χ²(2, N = 150) = 8.92, p = .012, Cramér’s V = .24.
chi-square.plainLanguage: Gender and preference are related. The pattern of preferences differs between men and women more than we would expect by chance.

correlation.paperReady: A Pearson correlation revealed a significant positive relationship between study hours and exam score, r(48) = .62, p < .001.
correlation.plainLanguage: Students who studied more hours tended to score higher on the exam. This is a moderately strong positive relationship.

descriptive.paperReady: The sample (N = 120) had a mean score of 72.45 (SD = 14.32), with scores ranging from 38 to 98. The distribution was approximately normal (skewness = -0.23, kurtosis = -0.41).
descriptive.plainLanguage: The average score was about 72 out of 100. Most scores fell between 58 and 87, with a fairly symmetric spread.

sample-size.paperReady: A priori power analysis indicated that a minimum sample of N = 128 (64 per group) is required to detect a medium effect (d = 0.50) with 80% power at α = .05 for an independent samples t-test.
sample-size.plainLanguage: You need at least 64 participants in each group (128 total) to have a good chance of detecting a meaningful difference if one exists.

one-sample-t.paperReady: A one-sample t-test indicated that the mean satisfaction score (M = 3.75, SD = 0.89) was significantly higher than the neutral midpoint of 3.0, t(79) = 7.53, p < .001, d = 0.84.
one-sample-t.plainLanguage: Participants rated their satisfaction significantly above the neutral point, suggesting overall positive sentiment with a large effect.

mann-whitney.paperReady: A Mann-Whitney U test indicated that scores were significantly higher in the treatment group (Mdn = 45) than in the control group (Mdn = 38), U = 892, p = .003, r = .31.
mann-whitney.plainLanguage: The treatment group had meaningfully higher scores than the control group, even when accounting for the non-normal distribution of the data.

wilcoxon.paperReady: A Wilcoxon signed-rank test indicated that post-intervention scores (Mdn = 82) were significantly higher than pre-intervention scores (Mdn = 71), T = 156, p = .002, r = .42.
wilcoxon.plainLanguage: Scores improved significantly after the intervention. The change was meaningful and unlikely to be due to chance.

regression.paperReady: Simple linear regression revealed that study hours significantly predicted exam scores, F(1, 48) = 27.34, p < .001, R² = .36, with each additional hour associated with a 3.45-point increase (β = .60).
regression.plainLanguage: Study hours explain about 36% of the variation in exam scores. Each extra hour of studying is linked to roughly 3.5 more points.

multiple-regression.paperReady: A multiple regression model with study hours, attendance, and prior GPA significantly predicted final exam scores, F(3, 96) = 18.42, p < .001, R² = .37. Study hours (β = .35, p < .001) and prior GPA (β = .29, p = .004) were significant predictors.
multiple-regression.plainLanguage: Together, study hours, attendance, and GPA explain about 37% of exam score differences. Study hours and prior GPA are the strongest predictors.

cronbach-alpha.paperReady: The 10-item scale demonstrated good internal consistency, Cronbach’s α = .85, 95% CI [.81, .89]. Item-total correlations ranged from .42 to .71.
cronbach-alpha.plainLanguage: The scale items measure the same underlying concept consistently. Reliability is good, meaning you can trust the total score.

logistic-regression.paperReady: Logistic regression indicated that study hours significantly predicted pass/fail status, B = 0.82, SE = 0.24, Wald χ²(1) = 11.67, p < .001, OR = 2.27, 95% CI [1.42, 3.63].
logistic-regression.plainLanguage: Each additional hour of studying more than doubles the odds of passing. Study time is a strong predictor of whether students pass or fail.

factor-analysis.paperReady: Exploratory factor analysis with varimax rotation yielded a 3-factor solution explaining 62.4% of the total variance. KMO = .84, Bartlett’s test χ²(45) = 312.7, p < .001.
factor-analysis.plainLanguage: The items group into three clear factors that together explain most of the variation in responses. The data is well-suited for factor analysis.

kruskal-wallis.paperReady: A Kruskal-Wallis H test showed a statistically significant difference in satisfaction scores across four departments, H(3) = 12.46, p = .006, ε² = .11.
kruskal-wallis.plainLanguage: Satisfaction levels differed significantly between departments. Follow-up pairwise comparisons can identify which departments differ.

repeated-measures.paperReady: A repeated measures ANOVA revealed a significant effect of time on anxiety scores, F(2, 58) = 14.23, p < .001, η²p = .33. Mauchly’s test indicated sphericity was met, χ²(2) = 3.12, p = .21.
repeated-measures.plainLanguage: Anxiety levels changed significantly over the three time points. The effect was large, explaining about 33% of the within-subject variation.

two-way-anova.paperReady: A 2 × 3 factorial ANOVA revealed a significant main effect of treatment, F(1, 114) = 8.92, p = .003, η²p = .07, and a significant interaction, F(2, 114) = 4.15, p = .018, η²p = .07.
two-way-anova.plainLanguage: Both the treatment type and its interaction with the other factor influenced the results. The effect of treatment depended on the level of the second factor.

friedman.paperReady: A Friedman test indicated a significant difference in preference rankings across the four products, χ²(3) = 18.45, p < .001, W = .31.
friedman.plainLanguage: People consistently ranked the products differently, suggesting genuine differences in preference rather than random variation.

fisher-exact.paperReady: Fisher’s exact test indicated a significant association between treatment group and outcome, p = .023, OR = 3.12, 95% CI [1.15, 8.47].
fisher-exact.plainLanguage: There is a significant relationship between treatment and outcome. Those in the treatment group were about 3 times more likely to have a positive outcome.

mcnemar.paperReady: McNemar’s test indicated a significant change in response proportions from pre-test to post-test, χ²(1) = 9.80, p = .002, OR = 3.50.
mcnemar.plainLanguage: Significantly more participants changed from negative to positive responses than the reverse after the intervention.

sampleSize.metaTitle: Free Sample Size Calculator Online - Power Analysis
sampleSize.metaDescription: Free sample size calculator for researchers. Power analysis for t-test, ANOVA, chi-square, correlation. Get the required N for your IRB proposal with instant APA results.
sampleSize.title: Sample Size Calculator
sampleSize.description: Calculate the required sample size for your study using power analysis. Supports t-tests, ANOVA, correlation, chi-square, and proportion tests.
sampleSize.inputDescription: Select your test type, effect size, significance level, and desired power.
sampleSize.errorEffectSize: Effect size must be greater than 0.
sampleSize.exampleScenario: A clinical researcher is planning an RCT to test a new anxiety medication. Using Cohen's d = 0.5 (medium effect), they need to determine the required sample size.

oneSampleT.metaDescription: Free online one-sample t-test calculator. Compare your sample mean to a known value with APA-formatted results, Cohen's d, and 95% CI.
oneSampleT.title: One-Sample T-Test Calculator
oneSampleT.description: Compare your sample mean to a known or hypothesized value. Results include t-statistic, p-value, Cohen's d, and 95% CI in APA format.
oneSampleT.testValueHint: The population mean or hypothesized value to compare against
oneSampleT.inputDescription: Enter sample data to compare against a known or hypothesized value.
oneSampleT.errorTestValue: Please enter a valid test value.
oneSampleT.exampleScenario: A quality control engineer tests whether the average weight of cereal boxes differs from the labeled 500g, measuring 12 boxes from the production line.

mannWhitney.metaTitle: Free Mann-Whitney U Test Calculator Online
mannWhitney.metaDescription: Free online Mann-Whitney U test calculator. Non-parametric test for comparing two independent groups with APA-formatted results.
mannWhitney.title: Mann-Whitney U Test Calculator
mannWhitney.description: Non-parametric alternative to the independent samples t-test. Compare two independent groups without assuming normal distribution.
mannWhitney.exampleScenario: A pain researcher compared pain ratings (1-10 ordinal scale) between patients receiving a new analgesic (n=8) vs placebo (n=8), where data is not normally distributed.

wilcoxon.metaTitle: Free Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test Calculator Online
wilcoxon.metaDescription: Free online Wilcoxon signed-rank test calculator. Non-parametric paired test with APA-formatted results. Compare pre/post measurements.
wilcoxon.title: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test Calculator
wilcoxon.description: Non-parametric alternative to the paired samples t-test. Compare two related measurements without assuming normal distribution.
wilcoxon.errorEqualLength: Pre and post arrays must have equal length.
wilcoxon.inputDescription: Enter pre-test and post-test measurements for each subject.
wilcoxon.exampleScenario: A hospital compared patient satisfaction ratings (1-5 Likert scale) before and after implementing a new care protocol for 10 patients.

regression.metaTitle: Free Simple Linear Regression Calculator Online
regression.metaDescription: Free online simple linear regression calculator. R-squared, F-test, coefficients, scatter plot with APA-formatted results.
regression.title: Simple Linear Regression Calculator
regression.description: Fit a linear model to your data. Results include R², F-test, regression coefficients, scatter plot, and APA-formatted output.
regression.errorEqualLength: X and Y must have the same number of values.
regression.inputDescription: Enter paired X and Y values to fit a linear regression model.
regression.scatterPlotDesc: Data points with fitted regression line
regression.exampleScenario: A marketing analyst examined how monthly advertising spend (in $1000s) predicts quarterly sales revenue (in $1000s) using data from 10 quarters.

multipleRegression.metaDescription: Free online multiple regression calculator. R², adjusted R², F-test, VIF, Durbin-Watson, standardized betas, ANOVA table with APA-formatted results.
multipleRegression.title: Multiple Regression Calculator
multipleRegression.description: Predict an outcome from multiple predictors using OLS regression. Results include R², coefficients with VIF, ANOVA table, and APA-formatted output.
multipleRegression.inputDescription: Enter outcome (Y) and predictor (X) variables. Add or remove predictors as needed.
multipleRegression.errorEqualLength: {name} must have the same number of values as Y.
multipleRegression.exampleScenario: An education researcher investigated whether study hours and attendance rate predict exam scores for 15 college students.

cronbachAlpha.metaTitle: Free Cronbach's Alpha Calculator — Item-Total & If-Item-Deleted
cronbachAlpha.metaDescription: Free Cronbach's alpha calculator with item-total correlation, alpha if-item-deleted, split-half reliability, and APA-formatted results. No signup required.
cronbachAlpha.title: Cronbach's Alpha Calculator
cronbachAlpha.description: Assess the internal consistency of a scale or questionnaire. Paste your data matrix (rows = participants, columns = items).
cronbachAlpha.highlightHint: Highlighted items would increase alpha if removed.
cronbachAlpha.errorInvalidMatrix: Invalid matrix. Each row must have the same number of values.
cronbachAlpha.exampleScenario: A psychologist assessed the internal consistency of an 8-item self-esteem scale (1-5 Likert) administered to 10 participants.

logisticRegression.metaDescription: Free online logistic regression calculator. Odds ratios, Wald test, classification table, Hosmer-Lemeshow test, and APA-formatted results.
logisticRegression.title: Logistic Regression Calculator
logisticRegression.description: Predict a binary outcome (0/1) from one or more predictors using logistic regression (IRLS). Results include odds ratios, classification table, and model fit statistics.
logisticRegression.inputDescription: Enter a binary outcome variable (Y: 0 or 1) and one or more predictor variables.
logisticRegression.convergenceWarning: Warning: The model did not converge. Results may be unreliable.
logisticRegression.separationWarning: Warning: Possible complete or quasi-complete separation detected. Some coefficients may be unstable.
logisticRegression.hosmerGoodFit: The model shows adequate fit (p > .05). The predicted probabilities match the observed frequencies.
logisticRegression.hosmerPoorFit: The model shows poor fit (p < .05). The predicted probabilities may not match the observed frequencies well.
logisticRegression.errorEqualLength: All variables must have the same number of values.
logisticRegression.errorNoPredictors: Enter at least one predictor variable.
logisticRegression.exampleScenario: A marketing analyst investigated whether age and income predict product purchase (yes/no) for 20 customers.

factorAnalysis.metaDescription: Free online exploratory factor analysis calculator. KMO test, Bartlett's test, eigenvalues, factor loadings with Varimax and Promax rotation, and APA-formatted results.
factorAnalysis.title: Factor Analysis Calculator
factorAnalysis.description: Perform exploratory factor analysis (EFA) with KMO test, Bartlett's test, eigenvalue decomposition, factor loadings, and communalities. Supports PCA and PAF extraction with Varimax and Promax rotation.
factorAnalysis.errorInvalidMatrix: Invalid matrix. Each row must have the same number of values.
factorAnalysis.exampleScenario: A researcher explores the factor structure of an 8-item personality questionnaire (N = 30). Three factors are expected: extraversion (items 1-3), conscientiousness (items 4-5), and openness (items 6-8).

kruskalWallis.metaTitle: Free Kruskal-Wallis H Test Calculator Online
kruskalWallis.metaDescription: Free online Kruskal-Wallis H test calculator. Non-parametric comparison of three or more independent groups with APA-formatted results.
kruskalWallis.title: Kruskal-Wallis H Test Calculator
kruskalWallis.description: Non-parametric alternative to one-way ANOVA. Compare three or more independent groups without assuming normal distribution. Results include H statistic, p-value, and pairwise comparisons.
kruskalWallis.groupMinValues: Each group needs at least 2 values.
kruskalWallis.exampleScenario: A nutritionist compared weight loss (kg) across 3 diet types: low-carb (n=8), Mediterranean (n=8), and intermittent fasting (n=8), where data is not normally distributed.

repeatedMeasures.metaTitle: Free Repeated Measures ANOVA Calculator Online
repeatedMeasures.metaDescription: Free online repeated measures ANOVA calculator. Within-subjects analysis with Mauchly's sphericity test, Greenhouse-Geisser correction, and APA-formatted results.
repeatedMeasures.title: Repeated Measures ANOVA Calculator
repeatedMeasures.description: Compare means across three or more conditions measured on the same subjects. Results include F-statistic, sphericity test, effect size, and post-hoc comparisons in APA format.
repeatedMeasures.sphericityMet: Sphericity assumption met (p > .05). Uncorrected F-test is reported.
repeatedMeasures.sphericityViolated: Sphericity violated (p < .05). Greenhouse-Geisser corrected values are reported.
repeatedMeasures.minConditions: Repeated measures ANOVA requires at least 3 conditions.
repeatedMeasures.conditionMinValues: Each condition needs at least 3 values.
repeatedMeasures.errorEqualLength: All conditions must have the same number of values (same subjects).
repeatedMeasures.exampleScenario: A cognitive psychologist measured reaction time (ms) of 10 participants under 3 lighting conditions: dim, normal, and bright.

twoWayAnova.metaDescription: Free online two-way ANOVA (factorial) calculator. Main effects, interaction effect, interaction plot, and APA-formatted results.
twoWayAnova.title: Two-Way ANOVA Calculator
twoWayAnova.description: Examine the main effects and interaction effect of two independent variables on a continuous outcome. Results include F-statistics, partial eta-squared, and interaction plot.
twoWayAnova.minLevels: Each factor needs at least 2 levels.
twoWayAnova.cellMinValues: Each cell needs at least 2 values.
twoWayAnova.errorUnequalCells: Unequal cell sizes detected. Type III sum of squares will be used.
twoWayAnova.exampleScenario: An education researcher examined the effects of teaching method (lecture vs. active learning) and class size (small vs. large) on exam scores for 40 students.

friedman.metaDescription: Free online Friedman test calculator. Non-parametric repeated measures comparison with APA-formatted results. Alternative to repeated measures ANOVA.
friedman.title: Friedman Test Calculator
friedman.description: Non-parametric alternative to repeated measures ANOVA. Compare ranks across three or more related conditions measured on the same subjects.
friedman.minConditions: Friedman test requires at least 3 conditions.
friedman.conditionMinValues: Each condition needs at least 3 values.
friedman.errorEqualLength: All conditions must have the same number of values (same subjects).
friedman.exampleScenario: A sommelier asked 10 judges to rate 4 wines (1-10 scale). The Friedman test checks whether there are significant differences in wine rankings across judges.

fisherExact.metaTitle: Free Fisher's Exact Test Calculator Online
fisherExact.metaDescription: Free online Fisher's exact test calculator for 2×2 contingency tables. Exact p-value, odds ratio, and APA-formatted results.
fisherExact.title: Fisher's Exact Test Calculator
fisherExact.description: Exact test for 2×2 contingency tables. Ideal when expected cell frequencies are less than 5 or sample sizes are small. Results include exact p-value, odds ratio, and confidence interval.
fisherExact.errorMinCells: Please fill in all 4 cells with valid non-negative integers.
fisherExact.errorAllZero: At least one cell must have a non-zero value.
fisherExact.exampleScenario: A clinical trial tested whether a new vaccine prevents infection. Of 15 vaccinated subjects, 2 got infected vs. 10 of 15 in the placebo group.

mcnemar.metaDescription: Free online McNemar test calculator for paired nominal data. Test proportion changes in before-after or matched-pair designs with APA-formatted results.
mcnemar.title: McNemar Test Calculator
mcnemar.description: Test for changes in proportions in paired nominal data. Used for before-after studies or matched-pair designs with binary outcomes.
mcnemar.errorMinCells: Please fill in all 4 cells with valid non-negative integers.
mcnemar.errorAllZero: At least one cell must have a non-zero value.
mcnemar.errorNoDiscordant: No discordant pairs found. McNemar test requires at least some change between conditions.
mcnemar.exampleScenario: A public health researcher surveyed 100 people about their opinion on a policy (favor/oppose) before and after watching an informational video.

wizard.title: Which Test Should I Use?
wizard.subtitle: Answer a few questions and we'll recommend the right statistical test.
wizard.q_how_many_groups: How many groups are you comparing?
wizard.q_paired_or_independent: Are the two groups independent or paired?
wizard.q_data_normal: Is your data approximately normally distributed?
wizard.q_dv_continuous_or_categorical: Is your dependent variable continuous or categorical?
wizard.q_predict_or_associate: Do you want to predict or just measure association?
wizard.r_independent_t_desc: Compares the means of two independent groups. Uses Welch's correction for unequal variances.
wizard.r_paired_t_desc: Compares means from the same participants measured twice (e.g., pre-test vs post-test).
wizard.r_mann_whitney_desc: Non-parametric alternative to the independent t-test. Works with ordinal data or non-normal distributions.
wizard.r_wilcoxon_desc: Non-parametric alternative for paired/one-sample comparisons when normality is violated.
wizard.r_one_sample_t_desc: Tests whether a sample mean differs significantly from a known or hypothesized value.
wizard.r_anova_desc: Compares means across three or more groups. Includes post-hoc tests to identify which groups differ.
wizard.r_kruskal_desc: Non-parametric alternative to ANOVA for non-normal or ordinal data. StatMate's ANOVA page includes assumption checks.
wizard.r_chi_square_desc: Tests relationships between categorical variables or whether observed frequencies match expected frequencies.
wizard.r_correlation_desc: Measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two continuous variables.
wizard.r_regression_desc: Models the relationship between a predictor (X) and outcome (Y) variable with an equation.
wizard.r_descriptive_desc: Summarizes your data with mean, median, standard deviation, and distribution charts.
wizard.r_sample_size_desc: Determine the required sample size for your study using power analysis. Supports t-tests, ANOVA, correlation, and more.
wizard.q_how_many_factors: How many factors (independent variables) are in your design?
wizard.q_expected_count_sufficient: Are all expected cell counts at least 5?
wizard.r_kruskal_wallis_desc: Non-parametric comparison of three or more independent groups. Alternative to one-way ANOVA when normality is violated.
wizard.r_repeated_measures_desc: Compares means across three or more conditions measured on the same subjects. Includes Mauchly's sphericity test and Greenhouse-Geisser correction.
wizard.r_two_way_anova_desc: Examines main effects and interaction of two factors. Produces separate F-tests for Factor A, Factor B, and their interaction.
wizard.r_friedman_desc: Non-parametric alternative to repeated measures ANOVA for three or more related groups with ordinal or non-normal data.
wizard.r_fisher_exact_desc: Exact test for 2×2 contingency tables when expected cell counts are small. Computes exact p-value, odds ratio, and confidence intervals.
wizard.r_mcnemar_desc: Tests for changes in paired binary data (before/after). Uses exact binomial test for small samples and chi-square approximation for larger ones.

newsletter.title: Stay Updated with Statistics Tips
newsletter.description: Get weekly tips on statistical analysis, APA formatting, and new calculator updates.
newsletter.success: Thanks for subscribing! Check your inbox for confirmation.

expertReview.title: Expert Statistical Review
expertReview.description: Get your analysis reviewed by a statistics expert with detailed feedback and APA-formatted suggestions.

features.f2: Detailed review by a PhD statistician

steps.s1: Submit your analysis results and research context through the form.
steps.s2: Complete payment via Lemon Squeezy (₩49,000 per review).
steps.s3: Receive detailed expert feedback via email within 48 hours.

expertReview.ctaBottomSub: Improve your research with professional statistical review.

form.pageDescription: Fill in the details below to request a statistical review.
form.dataPlaceholder: Describe your dataset: sample size, variables, collection method...
form.resultsPlaceholder: Paste your analysis output or describe your findings...
form.note: Payment link will be sent after form submission.
form.successMsg: We'll send payment details to your email shortly.

r1.title: PhD Statistician
r1.desc: 10+ years in quantitative research. Published in JASA and Psychometrika. Specializes in multivariate analysis and structural equation modeling.

r2.title: Research Methods Professor
r2.desc: 15+ years teaching at an R1 university. Member of the APA editorial board. Expert in experimental design and APA reporting standards.

items.0: Statistical methodology check — ensuring the right test for your research design
items.1: APA format verification — every table, figure, and in-text statistic reviewed
items.2: Interpretation accuracy review — confirming your conclusions match your results
items.3: Written feedback with suggestions — concrete, actionable improvements for your paper

guarantee.title: 100% Satisfaction Guarantee
guarantee.desc: If you're not completely satisfied with your review, we offer a free re-review or a full refund within 7 days. No questions asked.

0.q: How long does the review take?
0.a: Most reviews are completed within 48 hours. For complex analyses with multiple tests, it may take up to 72 hours. You'll receive an email notification as soon as your review is ready.

1.q: What types of analyses do you review?
1.a: We review all analysis types supported by StatMate — from basic t-tests and ANOVA to advanced methods like logistic regression, factor analysis, and repeated measures designs.

2.a: Yes! One follow-up question is included free of charge with every review. You can ask for clarification or additional guidance on any part of the feedback.

3.q: What if I need revisions to the review?
3.a: Our satisfaction guarantee applies. If the review doesn't meet your expectations, we'll revise it for free or provide a full refund within 7 days.

0.a: Annual subscription with a single invoice. Your institution gets one license that covers all students and faculty in the department. No individual seat management needed.

1.a: Absolutely! We offer a 30-day free trial for your entire department. All Pro features are unlocked during the trial period so you can evaluate the full experience.

2.a: Priority email support with guaranteed response within 24 hours, plus a free onboarding session for your faculty. We also provide documentation and tutorial materials for students.

compare.metaTitle: StatMate vs SPSS vs R vs Excel — Statistics Tool Comparison
compare.metaDescription: Compare StatMate with SPSS, R, and Excel for statistical analysis. See which tool is best for APA formatting, privacy, and ease of use.
compare.title: StatMate vs SPSS vs R vs Excel
compare.description: An honest comparison of popular statistics tools. Find the right one for your research needs.

whenStatmate.title: Choose StatMate when...
whenStatmate.text: You need quick, accurate results with automatic APA formatting. Ideal for students, thesis writers, and researchers who want publication-ready output without learning complex software.

whenSpss.title: Choose SPSS when...
whenSpss.text: You need advanced procedures (SEM, HLM, survival analysis) or your institution provides a license. Best for researchers with complex data analysis needs.

whenR.title: Choose R when...
whenR.text: You need maximum flexibility, custom analyses, or reproducible research pipelines. Best for data scientists and statisticians comfortable with programming.

whenExcel.title: Choose Excel when...
whenExcel.text: You only need basic descriptive statistics or simple charts. Not recommended for inferential statistics or academic research.

compare.ctaDescription: No installation, no sign-up. Get APA-formatted results in seconds.

validation.metaDescription: Cross-validation results comparing StatMate calculators against R 4.3 and SPSS 29. All 20 calculators verified to 4+ decimal place accuracy.
validation.title: Validation & Accuracy
validation.description: Every StatMate calculator is cross-validated against R 4.3.x and SPSS 29 using identical datasets. Below you'll find detailed comparison results for all 20 calculators, so you can cite StatMate with confidence in your thesis or publication.
validation.tableDesc: Each calculator was tested with representative datasets. R output and StatMate output are compared side-by-side.
validation.tableNote: All p-values rounded to 4 decimal places for display. Internal computations use full floating-point precision via jStat and simple-statistics libraries.
validation.methodDesc: StatMate uses well-established open-source statistical libraries, each tested against reference implementations in R 4.3.x. Our validation process ensures that every calculator produces results identical to R at 4+ decimal places.
validation.methodCustom: Custom implementations for specialized tests (Friedman, McNemar, Factor Analysis), validated against R output
validation.methodStep1: Generate test datasets with known properties in R 4.3.x
validation.methodStep2: Run identical analyses in both R and StatMate
validation.methodStep4: Repeat with edge cases (small n, extreme values, ties)
validation.citationText: If you use StatMate for your thesis, dissertation, or publication, you can reference its validation methodology as follows:
validation.citationValidationExample: Statistical analyses were performed using StatMate (https://statmate.org), a web-based calculator validated against R 4.3.x. All test statistics and p-values were verified to match R output to four decimal places across 20 statistical tests.
validation.ctaDesc: All 20 calculators are free, require no sign-up, and produce APA 7th edition formatted results.
